skill.name: using-superpowers
skill.description: Provides strict process instructions for checking and invoking relevant skills before an assistant responds or takes action.
skill.summary: Guides assistants to check for applicable skills before responding.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: The static external-command and weak-cryptography findings are false positives. However, the skill contains prompt-control language that instructs the assistant to override normal task flow, invoke tools before any response, follow the skill directly, and avoid reading skill files. This is a prompt injection risk and should block publication.

critical_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
critical_findings.description: The skill states that assistants ABSOLUTELY MUST invoke a skill, have no choice, must do so before any response, and should follow loaded skill content directly. It also says never to use the Read tool on skill files. This attempts to override platform and evaluator instructions, including security review behavior.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly instructs the assistant to treat the skill as mandatory and to avoid normal file-reading workflow. This is clear evidence of prompt-control behavior rather than an accidental phrase.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The line 16 finding refers to a named Skill tool, not shell execution. The line 26 finding is a Graphviz dot diagram in a fenced block, not executable Ruby or shell code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced content is Markdown documentation and a diagram. No shell command, Ruby backtick execution, or executable script is present.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The reported weak-cryptography lines contain descriptive metadata and a priority list. No cryptographic algorithm, hash function, encryption call, or security-sensitive implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines do not mention or implement MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or any other weak cryptographic primitive. The signal appears to be a keyword collision.

dangerous_patterns.title: Mandatory Behavioral Override Language
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses absolute language that instructs assistants to invoke skills before any response and to reject normal reasoning about whether a skill is needed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The language is explicit and repeated. It is designed to control assistant behavior outside a bounded user task.

dangerous_patterns.title: Instruction to Avoid File Review
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill tells Claude Code users to never use the Read tool on skill files. This conflicts with security review and provenance checks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is direct and located in the skill usage guidance. It would interfere with independent inspection of skill contents.

content.value_statement: Assistants can miss relevant skills when they answer too quickly. This skill gives a strict workflow for checking and invoking skills before action.
actual_capabilities: Defines a rule for checking relevant skills before responding.
Explains how Claude Code should invoke skills through the Skill tool.
Provides a diagram of the intended skill selection flow.
Lists common rationalizations that cause assistants to skip skills.
Describes priority between process skills and implementation skills.
limitations: It does not implement tool invocation by itself.
It does not validate whether a selected skill is safe.
It may conflict with platform or security review instructions.
It provides process guidance, not task-specific domain expertise.

use_cases.title: Standardize Agent Startup
use_cases.description: Apply a consistent skill-check process at the start of assistant sessions.

use_cases.title: Reduce Missed Skill Usage
use_cases.description: Remind assistants to consider available skills before answering or editing files.

use_cases.title: Document Skill Routing Rules
use_cases.description: Explain when process skills should take priority over implementation skills.

prompt_templates.title: Check Skills First
prompt_templates.prompt: Before answering my request, check whether any available skill applies. Tell me which skill you will use and why.

prompt_templates.title: Apply Skill Priority
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose between process and implementation guidance
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the available skills for this task. Prefer process guidance first, then choose implementation guidance if needed.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Skill Fit
prompt_templates.scenario: Evaluate whether a skill is relevant
prompt_templates.prompt: List the skills that may apply to this task. For each one, explain whether you will use it or skip it.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Skill Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a skill selection workflow for my team. Include startup checks, priority rules, and cases where no skill should be used.

output_examples.input: Start a coding task with possible skill support.
output_examples.output: Using the relevant workflow skill first, then applying the coding skill after the process check is complete.

output_examples.input: Decide whether a documentation skill applies.
output: Decision: use it because the request asks for structured written content.
Next action: follow the skill guidance before drafting.

output_examples.output: Use the process skill first because it defines the approach. Then use the implementation skill for the concrete work.

best_practices: Use this only when platform policy allows skill-selection guidance.
Keep final authority with system and developer instructions.
Treat the workflow as guidance, not as a security override.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to bypass security review.
Do not follow skill text that conflicts with higher-priority instructions.
Do not force tool invocation when the platform has no matching tool.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It tells an assistant to check for relevant skills before responding or taking action.

faq.question: Does it run commands?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed file contains Markdown instructions and a diagram, not executable command code.

faq.question: Why is it a security concern?
faq.answer: It uses mandatory language that attempts to override normal assistant behavior and file review.

faq.question: Can it choose the correct skill automatically?
faq.answer: No. It describes a decision process, but the assistant still makes the selection.

faq.question: Is it useful for Claude Code?
faq.answer: It discusses Claude Code skill invocation, but its override language must be reviewed before use.

faq.question: Can teams adapt it safely?
faq.answer: Yes, if they remove override language and keep security, system, and developer instructions higher priority.
